beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The five stages run denial, anger, bargaining, depression, then acceptance — the AI gambler who generates many and picks one. Exit denial by treating AI as probabilistic, not deterministic: default to asking every prompt for three different versions. Exit anger by managing the output like a manager: correct it, and add 'ask me clarifying questions' instead of quitting. Stage three is the perfect-prompt fallacy; the truth is volume beats precision, so generate many answers and choose the best.

artifactLines: The five stages run denial, anger, bargaining, depression, then acceptance — the AI gambler who generates many and picks one.
Exit denial by treating AI as probabilistic, not deterministic: default to asking every prompt for three different versions.
Exit anger by managing the output like a manager: correct it, and add 'ask me clarifying questions' instead of quitting.
Stage three is the perfect-prompt fallacy; the truth is volume beats precision, so generate many answers and choose the best.

beats.narration_text: I recognize myself in stage three — I keep hunting for the perfect prompt instead of generating volume. I want to move to the 'AI gambler' acceptance stage deliberately. Using my real task of writing cold outreach emails: (1) show me how to phrase a single prompt that returns a hundred usable variations at once, (2) give me a fast scoring rubric so picking the best one doesn't take longer than writing it myself, and (3) explain how the 'ask me clarifying questions' correction fits in once I've picked a winner.

props.command: I recognize myself in stage three — I keep hunting for the perfect prompt instead of generating volume. I want to move to the 'AI gambler' acceptance stage deliberately. Using my real task of writing cold outreach emails: (1) show me how to phrase a single prompt that returns a hundred usable variations at once, (2) give me a fast scoring rubric so picking the best one doesn't take longer than writing it myself, and (3) explain how the 'ask me clarifying questions' correction fits in once I've picked a winner.

beats.narration_text: The five stages, and the line that places you in each. Stage one, denial: AI gave you one bad answer and you've been telling that story ever since — or you paste outputs straight into client work because the AI said so. Stage two, anger: you screenshot bad AI outputs and post them. Stage three, bargaining: you think the perfect prompt is the answer. Stage four, depression: six hours at the keyboard and nothing to show. Stage five, acceptance — the AI gambler: you generate a hundred answers, pick the best one, and the job is done.

props.artifactTitle: Five stages — where do you fall?
artifactLines: Stage 1 Denial: 'AI is overhyped.' — One bad answer. Stopped there.
Stage 2 Anger: 'Look at this stupid answer ChatGPT gave me.'
Stage 3 Bargaining: 'I just need the perfect prompt.' ← Most people live here.
Stage 4 Depression: '6 hours. Nothing.' | Stage 5: Generate 100. Pick the best.
props.sparkLine: Five stages. Most stop at three.

beats.narration_text: How to exit stage one. The root problem is treating AI output as deterministic — one prompt, one answer, done. AI is probabilistic. Open a second chat with the exact same prompt and you'll get something different. That's how it works. The fix: default to asking for three versions in every prompt. Give me three different angles on this task for this success criteria. You'll never go back to one-shot prompting once you see what options look like.

props.title: Stage 1 exit — ask for options
props.code:
BAD: Write me an email to reschedule the meeting. GOOD: Give me 3 different angles on this email
to reschedule the meeting with [contact].
For each: a different tone, a different opening line.
Success criteria: they say yes.

beats.narration_text: Stage two exit: stop being angry at the output and start managing it. The point is precise. AI isn't a truth machine — it's an intern that follows your lead. When it gives you something wrong, you don't quit, you correct. A useful follow-up: 'You missed the point. I meant to do this. I want to make sure you understood — ask me clarifying questions.' That one follow-up beats ninety percent of magic prompts, because it treats AI like the junior it is and you like the manager you are.

props.title: Stage 2 exit — call out the miss
props.code:
BAD: [close the tab, post angry screenshot] GOOD: You missed the point. I meant to [call out
the specific mistake]. I want to make sure you understood.
So ask me clarifying questions.
props.sparkLine: Call out the miss. Follow up.

beats.narration_text: Stage three is where most people are stuck. The perfect prompt fallacy. The truth is that volume beats precision. The AI gambler at stage five doesn't write a better prompt — they run more prompts, generate more options, and pick the best one. The concrete move: stop trying to optimize a single prompt and start thinking in batches. Ask for five options. Regenerate the weak sections. The output you're trying to coax out of one perfect prompt is already sitting inside the batch — you just have to pull the lever enough times.

artifactLines: Stop optimizing one prompt. Start generating batches.
Ask for 5 options instead of 1. Regenerate the weak sections.
The best output is already in the batch — you have to pull enough times.
Stage 5 means: 100 outputs, pick the best, done. That's the whole game.

beats.narration_text: Stage four is depression — six hours at the keyboard and nothing useful to show. In practice, most people burn out here and quit AI entirely. The exit is counterintuitive: stop trying to get one great output and start generating output volume on purpose. Stage five is acceptance — the AI gambler. They run a hundred prompts, keep the five best, throw the rest away. The ratio is the whole point. The gambler doesn't treat a bad output as failure. They treat it as a pull that didn't hit. The next one might.

artifactLines: Stage 4: 6 hours, nothing. Exit: stop chasing one great output.
Stage 5: generate 100, keep 5, discard 95. That's the ratio.
A bad output isn't failure — it's a pull that didn't hit. Next.
The gambler has no attachment to any single output. That's the unlock.
props.sparkLine: Bad output is just a miss.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Run the slot machine on the actual task you're procrastinating on right now.

props.command: Give me 5 different versions of [the thing I've been procrastinating on — email / outline / proposal / draft]. Each one should take a different angle. I'll pick the best and we'll refine from there.
